revision.text:
[[ja:一塚古墳]] {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Round Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Hitotsuka Kofun | location = Shikano 1-chome, Taihaku-ku, Sendai, Miyagi Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|38.230333333333|N|140.875125|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | type = Circular Kofun (non-existent) | built = Around the 6th century | area = Diameter: 20–30 meters (estimated) | Interred_individual = Unknown | notes = None | burial goods = Sarcophagus, bronze mirror, magatama, glass beads, gold ring | image = [[File:Chomonkyo-HitotsukaKofun.jpg|250px]]
Bird-motif bronze mirror excavated from Hitotsuka Kofun
[[File:HitotsukaKofunAto.jpg|250px]]
Signboard marking the kofun’s location }} '''Hitotsuka Kofun''' (一塚古墳) was a {{ill|circular Kofun|ja|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/circular Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Round Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} located in Shikano 1-chome, Taihaku-ku, Sendai, Miyagi Prefecture. Estimated to have had a diameter of 20–30 meters, it was constructed during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|qid=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, around the 6th century. Excavations in 1906 revealed a {{ill|Sarcophagus|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}}, along with a {{ill|Bronze mirror|en|Bronze mirror|qid=Q1041984|lt=bronze mirror|1=Bronze mirror}}, magatama, glass beads, and a gold ring. The mound was subsequently destroyed, and no traces remain today. == location, Structure, and burial goods == [[File:一塚古墳出土 石棺.JPG|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Sarcophagus}}{{small|Displayed at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}.}}]] The mound was built on an alluvial plain south of {{ill|Ōtōjiyama|ja|大年寺山|1=Mount Daidenji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtōjiyama|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Daidenji|qq=}}, southwest of central Sendai. Approximately 300 meters west was {{ill|Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuzuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|lt=Futatsuka Kofun|ja|二塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futatsuka Kofun (Sendai)|qq=}}, while {{ill|Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)|ja|兜塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabutozuka Kofun (Sendai)|qq=}} lay northeast and {{ill|Uramachi Kofun|ja|裏町古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uramachi Kofun|qq=}} to the southwest. Hitotsuka Kofun is thought to have been constructed later than these, around the 6th century.Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," p.66; Ujiie Kazunori, "Kofun of Miyagi," pp.338-339; Fujisawa Atsushi, "Hitotsuka Kofun," p.258. The mound's diameter has been variously estimated as 36 meters, 22–23 meters, or 15 meters, with a height of around 3.6 meters.Takano Matsujirō estimated a diameter of 12–13 ken and a height of 2 ken, while other sources such as Fushise Chizō and Kasai Shinya offer differing figures. While some speculated it might be a keyhole-shaped mound, the consensus identifies it as a circular Kofun.Fushise Chizō, "A Record of Ancient Kofuns in Mogasakimura, Natori District," p.89; Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," p.62. No surrounding moat or {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} fragments were discovered.Takano Matsujirō, "Kofun Discovered Near Mogasakimura, Sendai," p.85. In April 1906, an excavation uncovered a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} beneath the mound, approximately 60 cm below the surface. The chamber was constructed by piling stone fragments and covering them with five stone slabs."Stone Coffin from a Kofun in Mogasakimura, Natori District," p.444. Inside the chamber was a {{ill|house-shaped Sarcophagus|ja|家形石棺|1=House-shaped Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/house-shaped Sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/House-shaped Sarcophagus|qq=}}, notable for its detailed construction. The coffin had a rectangular hollowed interior and a slanted roof-like lid. Measuring 238 cm in length, 90 cm in width, and 58 cm in height, the coffin featured short, cylindrical projections on its sides, possibly for attaching ropes.Fujisawa Atsushi, "Hitotsuka Kofun," p.258. Traces of red pigment were found on the coffin's interior and lid.Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," pp.62-63. burial goods recovered included a {{ill|bird-motif bronze mirror|ja|鳥文鏡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bird-motif bronze mirror|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (16.1 cm in diameter), five magatama, numerous glass beads in shades of blue and green, six small beads made of {{ill|Talc|en|Talc|qid=Q134583|lt=steatite|1=Talc}}, and a gold ring. The bronze mirror was heavily worn, indicating extended use, and was initially identified as an imported item from the late Eastern Han dynasty.Itō Nobuo, "Studies in Sendai's Local History," p.22. Later research suggested it might be a domestic product. The glass beads numbered 438 blue and 20 green, while the steatite beads measured approximately 4 cm in diameter.Takano Matsujirō, "Kofun Discovered Near Mogasakimura, Sendai," p.86. == Excavation and Current Status == The mound was known locally as "Hitotsuka" and "Futatsuka" as early as the Edo period, with no associated legends.Takano Matsujirō, "Kofun Discovered Near Mogasakimura, Sendai," p.85. By the early 20th century, the site was under cultivation.Fushise Chizō, "A Record of Ancient Kofuns in Mogasakimura, Natori District," p.89. On April 6, 1906, the mound's eastern section was excavated to extract soil, revealing the Sarcophagus. Waterlogged at the time of discovery, the coffin yielded significant burial goods. The items were later sent to the Imperial Museum, and the coffin was donated to the Dōda-bō Exhibition Hall, although it was temporarily lost before being recovered.Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," pp.61-62. Today, the coffin and many burial goods are housed at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}, while the bronze mirror is displayed at the [[:en:Tohoku History Museum|Tohoku History Museum]]. By 1950, the site had been leveled for housing development, with the {{ill|Keseyaba Works Dormitory|ja|萱場製作所|1=KYB Corporation|lt=KYB Corporation dormitory|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keseyaba Works Dormitory|13=User:Immanuelle/KYB Corporation|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カヤバ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11294875}} occupying the area.Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," p.61. The surrounding area is now a residential neighborhood, with only an intypeation board erected by the Sendai City Board of Education to commemorate the kofun. == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * Itō Nobuo, "Ancient Sites in Sendai," in Sendai City History, vol. 3, edited by the Sendai City History Compilation Committee, 1950. * Itō Nobuo, "Studies in Sendai's Local History," Hōbundō, 1979. * Ujiie Kazunori, "Kofun of Miyagi," in Studies of Miyagi, vol. 1, 1984. * Kasai Shinya, "An Overview of Protohistoric Sites in the Ōu Region," Archaeological Journal, vol. 8, no. 6, February 1918. * Takano Matsujirō, "Kofun Discovered Near Mogasakimura, Sendai," Archaeological World, vol. 6, no. 2, December 1906. * Fushise Chizō, "A Record of Ancient Kofuns in Mogasakimura, Natori District," Archaeological World, vol. 6, no. 2, December 1906. {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|一塚古墳|version=90075013|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:一塚古墳]] {{wikidata link|Q11352506}} [[Category:History of Taihaku Ward]] [[Category:Kofun of Miyagi prefecture]] [[Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] [[Category:Round Kofun]] [[Category:Taihaku-ku, Sendai]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
